blurb: An airy native daisy that hazes a xeric border with hundreds of small golden suns from midsummer until hard frost — wiry, see-through, and one of the longest-blooming wildflowers for hot, lean ground.
seasons: A wiry, airy plant smothered in small yellow daisies from midsummer until hard frost; dies back in winter
wildlife: Long-season nectar for bees and butterflies; small seeds for birds
deer: Not deer-resistant — deer and elk browse the leaves and flower heads

shots.cap: Summer — a bright golden daisy · © Anders Hastings (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Fall — the airy cloud of yellow daisies · © skynat303 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — daisies on fine wiry stems · © anne-outdoors (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: A tough perennial forb of the western mountains and high plains, recommended for USDA zones 4b–10a, so it is fully hardy in Boulder (zone 5b–6a) and dies back to a woody taproot each winter.[1] Its native range spans 4,430–9,840 ft in sagebrush, juniper, aspen and spruce-fir country, so high-altitude sun and cold are no problem.[1]
care.planting: Set out container-grown plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall while soil is workable; water in to establish, then taper off. It self-sows where happy, so siting it in a lean, open spot keeps it from looking weedy. In pots use a gritty, fast-draining mix, water more often (containers dry fast in our heat and wind), and protect the crown from winter wet, which rots the taproot.[1]
care.sun: Full sun — it wants the open, unshaded exposure of its native sagebrush and meadow habitat, and our intense Front-Range sun suits it well.[1]
care.soil: Well-drained loam to loamy sand; it grows naturally on lean, gravelly mountain soils and tolerates Boulder's alkaline clay so long as drainage is sharp. Avoid rich, heavily amended, or wet ground.[1]
care.water: Low; xeric once established. It is native to sites receiving only ~18–25 in. of annual precipitation, close to Boulder's rainfall, so give occasional deep soakings the first season and little to no supplemental water after.[1]
care.spacing: Give plants room — the wiry, branching stems reach ~3–4 ft and a single plant fills a 1.5–2 ft space; space roughly 18–24 in. apart in a border.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest from seed; the woody taproot makes mature plants hard to move, so divide only young clumps with care. Seed is dormant and needs pre-chilling (cold-moist stratification) to germinate, so the simplest route is a dormant fall sowing outdoors that lets winter break dormancy.[1]
care.sow: Dormant-seed in late fall, sowing 1/8–1/4 in. (0.3–0.6 cm) deep into a firm, weed-free bed, and let winter provide the cold pre-chill; seedling vigor is good and it can flower and set seed the first year.[1]
care.stratify: Seed has a pre-chilling (cold-moist stratification) requirement to break dormancy — supply it with a fall outdoor sowing or several weeks cold-moist in the refrigerator before a spring sowing.[1]
care.depth: Sow shallow — 1/8 to 1/4 in. (0.3–0.6 cm) deep.[1]
care.bloom: Long-blooming — small golden daisies from midsummer until hard frost, roughly July through October on the Front Range.[1][2]
care.feeding: None needed, and avoid high-nitrogen feeding: in nitrogen-rich ground (fertilizer or manure) showy goldeneye can accumulate nitrate to levels toxic to grazing livestock. Lean, unfertilized soil keeps it sturdy and safe.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low care: cut the spent wiry stems back in late winter before new growth, and edit out volunteer seedlings if it self-sows more than you want. Leave seedheads for birds.[1]
care.selfsow: Self-sows modestly and will spread by seed into open ground where it is happy; easy to thin, and welcome in a meadow or naturalistic planting.[1]
care.troubles: Largely pest- and disease-free; the main caution is nitrate accumulation when grown in high-nitrogen soil, a hazard to livestock rather than a garden disease.[1]

references.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guide — showy goldeneye (Heliomeris multiflora)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Heliomeris multiflora

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental and pollinator/wildlife plant, not for eating; no established culinary use and not known to be poisonous to people.[1]
edible.caution: Showy goldeneye is treated as inedible — the only food record is historical, the Goshute using the small seeds, and there is no established modern culinary use, so don't eat any part.[1] One real hazard: when grown in high-nitrogen ground (heavy fertilizer or manure) the plant can accumulate nitrate to levels that poison grazing livestock — a reason to keep it lean and unfertilized.[1] As with any wild daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, never eat one without certain identification.[1]
